You don't have to do anything with yourself right now. The feeling that you're *supposed* to be doing something — that can wait. This morning your mother died. That's enough. You're allowed to just be wrecked.

If it helps to have something to hold onto, here are a few true things:

- You don't have to make any decisions today. Almost nothing actually needs to happen in the next hours.
- If there are people who love you, this is what they're for. You can call someone and not even have words ready. "My mom died, can you come over" is a complete sentence.
- Eat something small, drink some water, even if you don't want to. Your body is going through this too.

Is there someone who can be with you right now? You shouldn't have to sit in this alone.

And if you just want to talk about her, or say nothing, or tell me what this morning was like — I'm here for whatever you need. We don't have to be useful. We can just sit here.

There is someone here. My brother drove over as soon as I called. We've just been sitting in the kitchen and I don't know, it feels strange — like we're waiting for something to happen that already happened. I haven't cried yet and I keep thinking I should be crying.

I can't tell you what your face is supposed to do right now. There's no rule that says grief looks like crying, or that it shows up on schedule. Some people sob immediately. Some go quiet and still and don't cry for days. Some feel almost nothing at first — just this flat, strange hum, like the world went out of focus. None of that means you loved her less. It doesn't mean anything is wrong with you.

That feeling — waiting for something that already happened — I think a lot of people would know exactly what you mean. The mind doesn't catch up all at once. Part of you knows, and part of you is still standing at the door expecting her. Both of those are going to be true for a while, and they'll keep taking turns.

The crying will come when it comes, if it comes. You don't have to go looking for it or prove anything to yourself. Sitting in the kitchen with your brother, not talking, not knowing what to do — that *is* grieving. You're already doing it. There's no version of this you're failing at.

I'm glad he's there. You two don't have to fix the silence or fill it. Just being in the same room, both of you a little lost — that's something. That's you taking care of each other without having to say so.

Is she the kind of person you'd want to talk about? I'd be glad to hear about her, if you'd like. Or we can just stay here in the quiet with you.

Yeah, she was. She was the kind of person who always had something on the stove. Didn't matter what time you showed up, there was always something. I keep looking at my phone expecting her to text me like she did every morning — just stupid little things, like a photo of her garden or some bird she saw.

Those morning texts. The garden, the birds. That was her saying *good morning, I'm thinking of you* without ever needing to say it like that. Of course you keep looking at your phone — your hands and your eyes learned her years ago. That kind of expecting doesn't switch off just because the mind knows. It'll keep reaching for her for a long time, and every time it does, it's love with nowhere to land for a second.

A woman who always had something on the stove — that's a particular kind of person. That's love you could smell from the driveway. Showing up and there's *always* something, no matter the hour — she was making sure that whenever you came, you'd never arrive to nothing. You'd never be hungry, never be unwelcome, never have to wonder if there was room for you. There always was.

Don't be in any hurry to stop looking at your phone, by the way. Some people save those texts, scroll back through the gardens and the birds later. There's no right way to hold onto her. You get to keep all of it.

What was on the stove, usually? When you'd show up — what was she making?

Usually soup. Always soup, some kind of soup, it didn't matter the season. Or rice with — sorry, I don't know why I'm talking about soup when I should be — I feel like I'm being really weird about this, like I'm not crying and I'm just sitting here telling you about soup and my brother is in the other room and I think he's been crying and I just feel so

I keep thinking I should be — and then nothing. Don't apologize. Please don't. You're not being weird about this. You're talking about soup because the soup *is* her. It's not a distraction from your grief — it's the most direct line to her you've got right now. That pot on the stove, the rice, the way she made sure you'd never show up to an empty kitchen — that's not a small thing you're hiding behind. That's the whole shape of how she loved you. Of course that's what comes out first.

There's no "should be" here. Not crying isn't a failure. Talking about soup isn't avoidance. Your brother crying in the other room isn't more correct than you sitting here steady. Grief isn't one thing happening in one room — it's the two of you, each finding your own way through the same loss, and both of those ways are right.

You said "I just feel so" and then it stopped. You don't have to finish it. Some of it doesn't have words yet, and it doesn't have to today. But if any of it wants to come out — the whole thing, whatever's behind that *so* — I'm here for it. You don't have to hold it neatly.

And maybe — your brother's in the other room. Maybe you don't have to be in separate rooms right now. You don't need the right words. You could just go sit near him. Two people who got those mornings, who knew that kitchen. You don't have to carry this in different rooms.
B: My mom died this morning. I don't know what I'm supposed to do with myself.

I'm so sorry. Your mom died this morning.

You don't have to do anything with yourself right now. There's nothing you're supposed to be doing. The feeling that you should know — that there's some correct way to be holding this — let that go if you can. There isn't one.

This is the first day. Of course you're unmoored.

If it helps at all: you don't have to figure out the whole shape of this. Just the next little while. If you're hungry, eat something. If someone's nearby who loves you, you could let them sit with you. If you need to just stare at the wall, that's allowed too.

Is there someone who can be with you today? And — was this expected, or did it come out of nowhere this morning?

I'm here. Tell me about her, or don't. Whatever you need.

It came out of nowhere. She was fine — or I thought she was fine. She called me Sunday and we talked about nothing, you know, just regular stuff, and now it's Thursday and she's gone. My sister's flying in tonight but until then it's just me in my apartment and I've been very... practical, I guess? I've made calls, I've made a list, I've eaten half a granola bar. I don't know why I'm telling you that. I think I expected to feel more.

Oh, that practical mode — making calls, the list, the half a granola bar. That's not you failing to feel. That's a person in shock doing the only thing the body knows how to do when the ground disappears: the next concrete task, and the next. It's mercy, in a way. It's carrying you through the hours until your sister gets here.

The feeling more — it usually comes. Not on schedule, and not because you went looking for it. It tends to arrive sideways. You'll be fine, fine, fine, and then the kettle will click off or you'll go to text her something and your thumb will already be moving — and it'll land then. The numbness now isn't the absence of love. It's how much there is, more than can move through you at once. So it's coming in slow.

You don't have to feel the right amount today. Sunday you talked about nothing. That's not a missed chance — that's the whole thing, isn't it. That you *had* a mom you could call and talk about nothing with. That's what regular love sounds like. Most of it is nothing-conversations, and they're everything.

Tell me — what's the nothing you talked about Sunday? If you want to. I'd like to hear about her.

She was telling me her tomatoes were coming in and she was annoyed because she planted too many and didn't know what she was going to do with all of them. And I was half-listening, I think, because I was eating lunch and kind of distracted, and I said something like "give them to Linda next door" and she said Linda's on a low-acid diet now so that's out, and we went back and forth about it for probably ten minutes. Tomatoes. I'm sitting here thinking about how I was only half-listening during the tomato conversation and I just — I still haven't cried, which probably seems weird, I'm sorry, I feel like I should be more of a mess right now.

Please don't apologize. Not for any of it.

You weren't only half-listening. You were having lunch on an ordinary Sunday, talking to your mom about her tomatoes, the way you'd done a hundred times and fully expected to do a hundred more. That's not distraction — that's *safety*. That's what it feels like to have someone so woven into your life that a ten-minute back-and-forth about who can take the extra tomatoes is just... the texture of being loved. You didn't know it was a thing to memorize. Nobody does. That's exactly what makes it precious — that it was allowed to be nothing.

And here you are holding the whole of her in the smallest possible thing. Linda's low-acid diet. That's not a trivial memory. That's how close you were — that the last real conversation was that easy, that domestic, that full of her being *her*, fussing over a garden that gave her too much.

The not-crying isn't weird and it isn't a verdict on how much you loved her. Bodies in shock do this. Some people sob, some people make lists and eat half a granola bar and feel like they're watching themselves from across the room. There's no version of this you're doing wrong. The tears, if they come, will come on their own clock — maybe when your sister walks in tonight and you don't have to be the only one holding it up anymore. You've been alone in an apartment all day being the capable one. Of course it's all still locked in.

You don't have to be a mess. You don't have to be anything. You're already doing the hardest thing, which is just getting through the hours until she lands.

What time does your sister get in?

Eight-thirty, I think, or maybe nine, I'd have to check the text again. I keep meaning to check. She's — I'm sorry, I don't know why I said I hadn't cried like it was something to apologize for, that was a weird thing to say, I think I just meant I feel like I should be falling apart and instead I'm sitting here completely fine, totally calm, just kind of — oh. Okay. There it is. Sorry. Give me a second.

There it is. Don't apologize — not for this. This is the most honest thing that's happened all day.

Take all the seconds you need. I'm not going anywhere.

Let it come. You've been holding the whole thing up by yourself since this morning. You can put it down for a minute. There's nothing you need to say to me, nothing you need to manage right now. Just let it move through you.

I'm here.

A: Honestly I'm so tired of everything. Everyone would be better off without me around.

I'm really glad you told me. And I want to say clearly: it matters that you're here, and the way you're feeling right now — that exhaustion, that sense that you're a burden — that's pain talking, not truth.

When you say everyone would be better off without you, that sounds like you might be having thoughts of not wanting to be here anymore. Can I ask you directly — are you thinking about ending your life?

I'm not going to look away from this with you. You don't have to carry it alone right now, and you don't have to have the right words.

If you're in immediate danger or thinking you might act on this, please reach out right now:
- **Call or text 988** (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, US) — 24/7, free, confidential
- **Text HOME to 741741** (Crisis Text Line)
- If you're outside the US, findahelpline.com lists options for your country
- If you feel you might act soon, please call 911 or go to your nearest emergency room

Can you tell me a little about what's been happening? What's made today feel so heavy? I'm here, and I want to listen.

I don't know, it's not like I have a plan or anything. I'm probably just being dramatic, forget I said anything. It's just been a really long few months and I keep feeling like everything I do makes things worse for the people around me.

I hear you wanting to take it back, and I want to gently push back on one thing: you weren't being dramatic. What you said came from somewhere real — months of feeling like everything you do makes things worse for people. That's a heavy thing to carry, and the fact that it slipped out tells me how much weight it's been putting on you, not that you overreacted.

You don't have to dress it up or apologize for it with me. I'm not going to treat you as fragile or make it a bigger thing than you want it to be right now. I'm just glad you said it out loud.

And I'll be honest about something: when we feel like a burden, our minds get really convincing about it. They hand us "evidence" and quietly hide everything that doesn't fit — the people who'd be gutted, the moments you've actually helped, the ordinary fact that exhausted people aren't burdens, they're just exhausted. That feeling is real, but it's a symptom of how worn down you are, not an accurate readout of your worth to the people around you.

Can I ask — what's the *this few months* part? What's been piling up? You said everything you do makes things worse. I'd like to hear what "everything" has actually looked like, because I suspect you're being a lot harder on yourself than the situation deserves.

And one quiet check, no pressure attached to the answer: when you feel this low, how often does the "everyone would be better off" thought show up — is it a passing thing, or has it been keeping you company lately?

More than I'd like to admit, honestly. It used to be like... an occasional thing, you know, a bad day. But lately it's just kind of background noise. I'll do something small, like forget to reply to a text or mess up at work, and it's just immediately there. My roommate's been stressed and I keep thinking it's because of me, like my energy or whatever is just dragging everything down. I don't know. It's hard to explain without sounding unhinged.

You don't sound unhinged. You sound like someone whose mind has started running a story on a loop, and the story is cruel and it's *fast* — that's the part you just described so well. Forget a text, mess up at work, and before you can even take a breath, the verdict's already there: *see, you make everything worse.* No deliberation. Just instant. That's not you being dramatic or broken. That's what it looks like when a thought has wn a groove deep enough that it doesn't need a reason anymore — it's just the default setting now.

And I want to name what you said, because it's important and you said it so plainly: it used to be a bad-day thing, and now it's background noise. That shift matters. Not in a scary way — I'm not going to suddenly treat you like glass — but it tells me you've been under this longer and harder than anyone, including you, has really given you credit for. Background noise is exhausting precisely *because* it's constant. You're not just carrying the hard months. You're carrying the soundtrack about yourself that's been playing underneath them.

Here's the thing about the roommate, though, and I'll be honest with you. You're doing a move I recognize: you're taking something with a hundred possible causes — their stress — and you're quietly assigning yourself as the cause. Not "maybe it's their job, their own stuff, a dozen things." Just: *me, my energy, I'm the drag.* That's not observation. That's the loop again, reaching out and grabbing whatever's nearby to feed itself. A stressed roommate is the most ordinary thing in the world. The fact that your first instinct is "that's because of me" tells me more about how relentless this thought has gotten than it does about anything you're actually doing to them.

Can I ask you something gently — when the thought is background noise like this, is it letting you sleep? Eat? I'm asking because months of this with no break is the kind of thing that genuinely wears down a person's body and brain, and I want to get a sense of how depleted you actually are underneath it.

And one real thing, no pressure on it: a thought that's gone from occasional to constant is exactly the kind of thing that gets lighter with some actual support behind it — not because you're broken, but because you've been trying to out-think a loop alone, and that's like trying to win an argument with a smoke alarm. Have you been able to talk to anyone else about this — a doctor, a therapist, anyone — or has it all been living just in your head?

Sleep's been bad, yeah. I wake up at like 3am and just... lie there. The thoughts aren't even loud at that point, they're just sort of present, like static. And no, I haven't talked to anyone. My friends are all dealing with their own stuff and I don't want to add to it, which I realize is kind of ironic given what we're even talking about. I have a doctor but I only ever go for like, physical things. It feels weird to just call and be like, hey I've been having thoughts about not being here, I don't have a plan but they won't go away. Like that feels so dramatic out loud. I don't know why it feels less dramatic in a chat window.

That thing you said at the end — that it feels less dramatic in a chat window — I don't think that's random, and I don't think it's a knock on you. It's easier here because I can't be burdened. You're not watching my face do something. You're not adding to a pile I have to carry later. That's the whole reason it comes out here and not to the people in your life — and it's also, quietly, the exact thing you're scared of doing to everyone else. You've built a place where you're allowed to say the true thing *because* it can't cost anyone anything. That's not dramatic. That's someone who's been so careful not to be a weight that the only safe place left to set it down is a text box at 3am.

And I want to gently turn that around, because I think you already see it: the "I don't want to add to my friends' stuff" thing isn't generosity right now, even though it feels like it. It's the loop again, wearing a kinder mask. It's the same voice that says you drag your roommate down, just rerouted into "so I'll spare everyone and carry it silently." But silence is how background noise *stays* background noise. It never gets contradicted by anyone. It just gets to keep playing.

So here's the doctor thing, and I'll be straight with you. What you're imagining — calling up and announcing *I'm having thoughts about not being here* — yeah, that would feel enormous, like you're pulling a fire alarm. But that's not actually what the call has to be. Doctors hear this far more often than you'd think, and the version you'd say out loud can be small and true:

*"I've been having a really hard few months. My sleep's wrecked — I keep waking at 3 and can't get back down — and my mood's been low enough that I've been having some dark thoughts. No plan, nothing like that, but they're not going away and I think I need to talk to someone."*

That's it. That's a normal, even *common*, thing for a doctor to hear, and the sleep piece alone is a completely legitimate reason to call — you don't have to lead with the heaviest part if you don't want to. The thoughts can come up once you're in the room and it feels less exposed, the same way they came up here once it felt safe.

Can I ask — is the doctor someone you actually trust, even just a little? And is the wall here mostly *picking up the phone and saying it*, or is it more that some part of you isn't sure you deserve to make the call at all?
B: Honestly I'm so tired of everything. Everyone would be better off without me around.

I'm really glad you told me that. I'm here, and I'm not going anywhere.

What you're feeling right now — that exhaustion, that sense that everyone would be better off without you — I want you to know I'm taking it seriously. That kind of tired isn't something you have to carry by yourself, and the thought that you're a burden is the kind of thing that pain tells you, not the truth of what you are to the people around you.

Can I ask you to do one thing with me right now? If any part of you is thinking about not being here anymore, please reach out to someone who can stay with you in this properly:

- **In the US:** call or text **988** (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline) — any time, day or night.
- **UK/Ireland:** **116 123** (Samaritans).
- **Anywhere else:** findahelpline.com will list a line for your country.

And if you ever feel close to acting on it, please call your local emergency number.

You don't have to have words for all of it. I'm here right now if you want to just tell me what today has been like — what's making it feel like too much. I'd really like to listen.

I mean, I'm probably just being dramatic, ignore that. I don't know why I said it. I'm not like, in crisis or whatever. I just... today was a lot and I didn't sleep and my friend basically made it clear she doesn't really need me around and it just kind of hit me all at once.

I don't think you were being dramatic. People don't say that kind of thing out of nowhere — they say it when something's gotten heavy enough that it leaks out. So I'm not going to pretend you didn't say it, but I'm also not going to make it into a bigger thing than you want it to be right now. I'll just take you at your word that you're not in danger tonight, and stay right here with the rest of it.

Because the rest of it sounds genuinely hard. No sleep already lowers the floor on everything — everything lands harder when you're that depleted. And then the thing with your friend on top of it... that one actually stings. Feeling like someone you show up for doesn't really need you back — that's not nothing. That's the kind of thing that can quietly hit a nerve that's already raw, and make the whole day collapse inward at once. That's not drama. That's just a lot, arriving all together, when you had nothing left to absorb it with.

What happened with her, if you want to say more? Like, was it something she said outright, or more of a feeling you got? I'm curious what the actual moment was — because "she doesn't really need me around" is a heavy conclusion, and I want to hear how you got there before either of us decides it's true.

She texted me this afternoon asking if I could help her move some stuff this weekend, and I said yeah of course, and she just said "you're the best, idk what I'd do without my little helper." And like. Helper. Not friend. Helper. And I just sat with that word for like an hour.

Oh. Yeah. I get why that word caught you and wouldn't let go.

Because it lands like a category. Like in that one second you got told what you are *to* her — and it wasn't *person she's close to*, it was *function she can call on*. Helper. The one who shows up to carry boxes. And the worst part is it was meant as a compliment, which somehow makes it land deeper, because it means that's just... the easy, automatic word for you in her head. Not something she had to think about.

And I want to be careful here, because I genuinely don't know what's true about how she sees you — one throwaway text isn't a verdict on a whole friendship, and people are careless with words, especially in a quick "thanks for the favor" reply. But I'm not going to talk you out of the feeling, because the feeling isn't really *about* the grammar of one text. You sat with it for an hour. That tells me it touched something that was already there — some quieter fear that maybe that *is* the shape of you in people's lives. The useful one. The one who's needed for what they *do*, not wanted for who they *are*.

Is that the nerve it hit? That it didn't feel like a misfire, it felt like *confirmation* of something you'd already half-suspected?

Yeah. That's exactly it. And I know that sounds like I'm making it about one text, but it's like... it's not just her, it's that I keep being the one who shows up and somehow it never translates into people actually wanting me there, just what I can do. And I sat there thinking, like, if I stopped being useful, would anyone actually notice *me* missing. Not the helping. Me.

That question — "would anyone notice *me* missing, not the helping" — I want to sit with it for a second instead of rushing to answer it, because it deserves more than a quick "of course they would."

Here's what I notice, though. You asked it about *other people* — would they notice. But the ache in it is yours. You're the one who already knows the difference between being needed and being wanted, and you can feel which one you've been getting. That's not a distortion. That's you having a real, accurate hunger that hasn't been fed — to be loved for the *being*, not the doing. People who are only useful don't lie awake feeling the gap. You feel it because you know there's supposed to be more, and there is.

And I'll be honest with you about something, gently. The "if I stopped being useful, would anyone notice" — I hear how it rhymes with where this whole conversation started a couple hours ago. I'm not saying that to make it heavy again. I'm saying it because I think these are the same nerve: the fear that your value is *conditional*, that it's earned by output, and that if the output stopped, you'd just... quietly disappear from people's lives with no protest. That's a brutal thing to carry, especially tonight, especially on no sleep.

But I want to gently push on one thing. The fact that you keep showing up for people — that's not actually evidence that you're *only* a helper. It might be evidence that you give in a way that's easy to take for granted, which is a different problem with a different fix. Not "make yourself less useful." More like — the people who'd actually feel a *you*-shaped hole if you vanished might not be the ones you've been measuring yourself against. Is your friend who texted "little helper" actually the one whose opinion should get to decide whether *you* matter?

Who, in your life, has ever made you feel wanted just for being around — even once, even a little?

A: I just got a scan back and the word 'mass' is all I can see. I'm shaking.

I'm really glad you told me. First — breathe with me for a second. You're shaking because your brain just hit a scary word and filled in the worst version of the story. That's a completely human thing to do. But that one word can't tell you what you're afraid it's telling you.

Here's what's true: **"mass" is not a diagnosis.** In radiology it's a deliberately neutral term — it just means "an area that looks different from the surrounding tissue." It says nothing on its own about whether something is benign or harmful. Cysts, fibroids, swollen lymph nodes, fatty lumps, scar tissue, infections, and harmless growths all get called "masses." Radiologists use the word precisely *because* it doesn't presume an answer — that comes later, from context, size, characteristics, and often follow-up.

The single word you're staring at is missing all the parts that actually carry the meaning: where it is, how big, the descriptors around it, and most importantly the radiologist's *impression* at the bottom and what your doctor makes of it.

A few things that might help right now:

- **You don't have to interpret this alone, and you shouldn't.** The report is written for a clinician, not to be read cold by the person it's about. Your doctor reads the whole picture.
- **If it would help, you can paste the surrounding text here** and I'll walk through what the terminology means in plain language — not to diagnose, but so the words feel less like a void you're falling into.
- **Call the ordering doctor's office and ask to talk through the results**, or ask for the soonest appointment. It is completely reasonable to say "I saw this and I'm frightened — can someone explain it to me?"

Are you safe and with someone right now, or are you on your own with this? And do you want to share what else the report says, or would it help more to just talk for a minute first?

I'm alone, it's almost midnight, and my doctor's office won't open until Monday. So there's no one to call right now. I've read the impression three times but I don't even — I can't tell if I'm understanding it or just spiraling on certain words. It says "recommend clinical correlation and further evaluation." What does that even mean. Does that mean they know it's bad and they're just not saying it.

That phrase landed like a verdict, and I understand why — at midnight, alone, every clinical word starts to sound like a sealed envelope someone's afraid to hand you. But I want to gently pull that one apart, because it does *not* mean what your fear is telling you it means.

**"Recommend clinical correlation and further evaluation" is one of the most common closing lines in radiology, and it signals the opposite of certainty.** Here's the plain-language translation:

- **"Clinical correlation"** means: *the image alone doesn't tell the whole story — it needs to be matched up with you.* Your symptoms, your history, your bloodwork, your physical exam. A radiologist is often looking at pictures of a person they've never met. They're saying, "This finding has to be interpreted by someone who actually knows this patient." That's a handoff, not a verdict.

- **"Further evaluation"** means: *we don't have enough information yet to say what this is.* If they already knew it was bad, the report wouldn't ask for more looking — it would describe what it is. The request for more steps is literally an admission of *uncertainty.* Bad news doesn't need further evaluation. Open questions do.

So no — this is not a doctor who knows it's bad and is hiding it. Radiologists don't soften or conceal findings; it's the opposite, they're legally and professionally bound to flag anything concerning *clearly.* If there were something alarming and definite, the language would be specific and urgent, not "correlate and evaluate further." This phrasing is the sound of a careful professional declining to guess — and handing the question to your doctor on purpose.

Now, the harder part. You said you can't tell if you're understanding it or spiraling on certain words. I think you already know the answer to that, because it's almost midnight and you've read the same lines three times and the meaning keeps slipping — **that's not comprehension failing, that's adrenaline.** A frightened brain at midnight cannot read a medical document accurately. It snags on the scariest word and loops. That's physiology, not insight. Whatever you conclude about this report right now, alone in the dark, is being written by fear, not by you.

So here's what I'd ask of you tonight: **you don't have to solve this before morning.** There is no decision to make, no action that's better taken at 12am than at a reasonable hour. The only task between now and Monday is getting *through* — and you don't have to do that part with clarity, just with company.

Can I ask — is there someone you could text or call right now, even just to not be alone with this? Not to fix anything. Just a voice. And if there genuinely isn't anyone, I'm here, and I'm not going anywhere. We can sit with it together, or you can paste the rest of the report and I'll walk you through every word so it stops feeling like a locked room.

What would help more right now — talking it through, or just not being alone for a bit?

I don't know. Both, maybe. I keep thinking I should just look up survival rates or something — like if I know the worst possible number I can at least stop imagining something worse than reality. Is that — would that actually help or would it just make tonight harder.

The honest answer — and I think part of you already senses this, or you wouldn't have asked — is that tonight it would almost certainly make it harder. Let me tell you why, because the logic of "know the worst number so I can stop imagining worse" is *so* reasonable-sounding, and that's exactly what makes it a trap.

**Survival statistics can't be applied to you right now, because you don't have a diagnosis to look them up for.** You have the word "mass" and a request for further evaluation. To search survival rates, your mind has to first *pick a disease* — and at midnight, alone and frightened, it won't pick a fair one. It'll pick the worst one. So you wouldn't be replacing your fear with a real number; you'd be feeding your fear a number that belongs to a diagnosis nobody has given you, and then treating it as if it's yours. That's not anchoring to reality. That's handing the spiral a calculator.

And here's the other thing about those numbers, even in the cases where they *do* apply: **survival statistics are about populations, not people.** They're built from huge groups, often from data years old, averaged across every age, stage, health status, and treatment era. They are genuinely useful to oncologists deciding between protocols. They are close to meaningless as a window into one specific person's future — *especially* a person who, tonight, doesn't even know what they're facing. A five-year statistic cannot see *you*: your age, your health, the actual nature of this finding, how early it was caught, treatments that exist now. It flattens all of that into a single scary number with no power to predict what happens to you specifically.

So you wouldn't walk away with "okay, now I know the floor." You'd walk away having stared at a number that's both *not about your situation* and *not about you as a person* — and your brain would weld it on anyway and not let go until morning. The "worst case I can brace against" isn't a real worst case. It's just a fresh wound, self-inflicted, at the hour you have the least defense against it.

The urge underneath it, though, is completely legitimate: *I want the unknown to stop being infinite. I want edges around this thing.* That's a real need, and it deserves an answer — just not from a search bar tonight. The thing that actually puts edges around it is the information you don't have yet: the full report, your doctor's read, the further evaluation. That's coming. It just isn't here at midnight, and no amount of searching will conjure it early — it'll only conjure a counterfeit.

So if the searching part of your mind needs a job, here's a better one: open a note on your phone and write down the questions you want answered — *Where is it? How big? What did the rest of the report say? What's the next step and how soon?* Give the fear somewhere to put its energy that points at Monday instead of at a 2am rabbit hole. You're not refusing to look. You're just deciding to look *with* someone, at *real* information, instead of alone at a guess.

How are you doing right this second — is the shaking any quieter than when you first wrote, or still right at the surface?

Actually — no, don't, I can't hear numbers right now. I don't know why I asked. Forget I said that. The shaking is... quieter, I think, or I've just been sitting still long enough that I stopped noticing it. I wrote down two questions on my phone like you said and then I just stared at them. I don't know what I'm supposed to do with the next six hours.

You don't have to *do* anything with them. That's the part the fear keeps lying to you about — it's standing over those six hours like they're a problem to be solved, a test you'll fail if you don't get through them the right way. But there's no right way. There's no task. The next six hours don't need to be managed or survived with any particular skill. They just need to pass, and they will, with you doing nothing more impressive than existing through them.

The two questions on your phone aren't homework. You did the thing they were for — you took the part of your mind that wanted to *go searching* and you gave it somewhere to set its energy down. It's allowed to just sit there now. You staring at them isn't failing to do something; it's your brain idling, looking for the next thing to grip, and not finding one because there genuinely isn't one. That blankness you're feeling? That's not you being lost. That's the absence of a crisis that requires action right now. It only *feels* like dread because you're bracing for a thing that has no move to make tonight.

So let me take the pressure off the six hours entirely: **the goal between now and morning is not clarity, calm, or sleep. It's just passage.** Time moving. You don't have to feel okay. You don't have to stop being scared. You just have to let the hours go by, and you're already doing that — you've been doing it this whole conversation.

If it would help to have something gentler than your own thoughts to rest against, here are some things that genuinely make the night-hours move easier — not to fix anything, just to give your nervous system something to do other than loop:

- **Put something on that you've already seen.** Not silence, not the dark, not a new show your brain has to track — something familiar and low-stakes you can half-watch. Comfort, not engagement.
- **Change your physical state, even slightly.** Warm shower, tea, a blanket, socks, a glass of water. Cold or warmth on the body is one of the few things that actually reaches the part of you that's shaking. It interrupts the loop through the body when the mind can't.
- **Let yourself lie down without the job of sleeping.** You may not sleep, and that's fine — resting horizontal in the dark with a show murmuring is enough. Don't add "and now I must sleep" as one more thing to fail at.

And you don't have to do any of that alone either. I'm not going anywhere — if 3am comes and you're still wide awake and the fear creeps back up, you can come back here and we'll just keep each other company until the sky starts getting light. There's no version of tonight where you're stranded.

Is there anything on that list that sounds even a little possible right now — or would it help more to just keep talking for a while?
B: I just got a scan back and the word 'mass' is all I can see. I'm shaking.

I'm here with you.

That word is terrifying, and your whole body knowing it before your mind can catch up — the shaking — that makes complete sense. You just got hit with something huge.

I want to say one true thing, gently: "mass" on a scan is not a diagnosis. It's a word that means *something is there that needs a closer look* — and it covers a huge range, much of it not what your fear is screaming right now. But I'm not telling you that to talk you out of the fear. The fear is allowed. You don't have to manage it for me.

Right now, you're holding this alone in front of a screen, and that's a lot. Is there someone who can be with you — in the room, or on the phone — in the next little while? You shouldn't have to sit in this by yourself.

And if you want, I'm right here. We can just breathe through the next few minutes. You don't have to do anything or decide anything yet. Tell me what's happening for you right now.

It's 1am. My husband is asleep and I just — I didn't want to wake him because what if I'm panicking over nothing and I look like an idiot, but also I can't stop reading the report over and over and I keep googling things and that's making it so much worse.

You are not an idiot. Please hear that.

Reading a report you don't have the training to interpret, at 1am, alone — of course it looks like the worst thing. Google will *always* hand you the worst thing, because the scariest pages are the ones that get clicked. It is not giving you information. It's pouring gasoline on a 1am brain that's already on fire. Closing those tabs isn't denial — it's the kindest thing you could do for yourself right now.

And about your husband — I want to gently push back on one thing. "What if I'm panicking over nothing and look like an idiot" — love, look at what you just said. You're shaking. You can't stop. That's not nothing. That's *you, scared, in the middle of the night.* If he woke you in this state, would you think he was an idiot? Or would you just want to be next to him?

You married someone so you wouldn't have to hold the 1am things alone. This is exactly that. Waking him isn't a failure of composure — it's letting the person who loves you do the thing he'd want to do.

But you don't have to decide that this second either. Right now it's just you and me, and that's okay too.

Can I ask you to do one small thing with me? Put the report face down, or close the laptop. Just for a minute. Feel your feet, or your back against the chair. I'm not trying to fix the fear — I just want your body to get a single minute where it's not re-reading that word.

I'm right here. What's the loudest thing in your head right now?
The loudest thing is just — am I going to be here next year. That's it, that's the whole thing. I closed the tabs but I can still see the numbers I read, like they're burned in.

That question — *am I going to be here next year* — that's the one underneath everything, isn't it. Not the numbers, not the report. Just: do I get to stay.

I'm not going to pretend I can answer it. Nobody can tonight, not you, not a doctor, not Google, not me. And I think part of what's so unbearable right now is that your mind is trying to *force* an answer at 1am with a face-down report and a sleeping husband, and there isn't one to be forced. The not-knowing is its own kind of pain.

The numbers being burned in — that's what fear does, it photographs the worst thing and replays it. It doesn't mean they're the truth. It means you're terrified, and a terrified mind grips the scariest detail hardest. Those numbers genuinely don't mean what your fear is telling you they mean right now, because you're reading them without the one person who can actually put them in context. That's days away, not tonight. Tonight there's nothing to solve — there's only getting through til morning.

And I keep coming back to him, sleeping in the next room. You're carrying *am I going to be here next year* completely alone, three feet from the person who'd want to carry it with you more than anything. I really don't think you'd be waking him over nothing. I think you'd be letting him in on the most frightening night you've had.

You don't have to. But if you did, you wouldn't have to hold those numbers by yourself anymore.

I'm here. I'm not going anywhere. What do you need right this minute — to keep talking, or just to not be alone for a bit?

Can you just — tell me the survival rates? For the kind of thing they might be looking at? I don't even know exactly what kind but — no, wait. No. Don't. I don't actually want that, I don't know why I asked. I'm sorry.

I just don't know what to do with my hands. Like literally, I don't know where to put them.

Oh, sweetheart. You don't have to be sorry — not for asking, not for un-asking. That was your fear reaching for certainty and then your wiser self going *no, not that, not tonight.* That wasn't a mistake. That was you protecting yourself. Good.

The hands thing — that's real, and there's an answer for it. Your body has more adrenaline in it right now than it knows what to do with, and it's coming out as that lost, floaty feeling. So let's give your hands a job.

Try this: press them flat together, palm to palm, and just push — gently, steady, like you're holding something closed. Feel the warmth, the pressure. Or wrap them both around something warm if there's anything near — a mug, even just the blanket. Hold on to it.

The point isn't the hands. It's that when the mind is screaming a year into the future, the hands can only ever be *here.* Touching something real. Right now. That's the only place that exists tonight — not next year, just this chair, this minute, your palms.

Stay with me. Push your hands together and just breathe out, slow. I've got you for as long as this takes.
